model_narrative: We place eight distinct digit values, one in each circle of a figure, choosing which digit goes in which circle. Alongside the digit placed in each circle we track, for every adjacent pair of circles, which of the two holds the smaller value, and we track the assignment of each digit value to a circle. The objective is to minimize an accounting variable that totals the digits placed across all circles.

sets.name: c
sets.doc: the circles in the figure, each of which must hold one digit; also serves as the set of available digit slots since there are exactly as many digits as circles

sets.name: cc
sets.doc: an alias of the circle set, used as the second index when a relationship ranges over pairs of circles or over the eight candidate digit values; the position of a member in this ordering, counting from one, is the digit value it represents

sets.name: net
sets.doc: the pairs of circles that are adjacent in the figure, listed as ordered pairs; the relation is symmetric so each unordered adjacency appears twice, once in each direction

params.name: bign
params.doc: a large constant used to deactivate an adjacency difference inequality when the relevant ordering indicator selects the other direction; a scalar with value 9

params.name: gap
params.doc: the minimum required absolute difference between the digits placed in two adjacent circles; a scalar with value 2

params.name: lo
params.doc: the smallest digit value that may be placed in a circle; a scalar with value 1

params.name: up
params.doc: the largest digit value that may be placed in a circle; a scalar with value 8

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the digit value placed in each circle, an integer bounded between the smallest and largest allowed digit

vars.name: ll
vars.doc: an ordering indicator for an ordered pair of circles; it records which of the two adjacent circles holds the smaller digit, and only one direction of each adjacency can be active

vars.name: y
vars.doc: a digit assignment indicator that equals 1 when a circle is given the digit value corresponding to the second index and 0 otherwise

vars.name: dummy
vars.doc: an accounting variable holding the total of all placed digits

constraints.name: x[cc]
constraints.intent: For each adjacent pair of circles in a given direction, the digit in the second circle must stay below the digit in the first circle by at least the required difference, but only when the ordering indicator for that direction says the first circle holds the larger digit. When the indicator points the other way this requirement is loosened by the big constant so that it places no real limit.

constraints.name: x[cc]
constraints.intent: For each adjacent pair of circles in a given direction, the digit in the second circle must exceed the digit in the first circle by at least the required difference, but only when the ordering indicator for the reverse direction says the first circle holds the smaller digit. When that indicator points the other way this requirement is loosened by the big constant so that it places no real limit.

constraints.name: ll[c,
constraints.intent: For each adjacent pair of circles, exactly one of the two ordering directions is active, so between any two adjacent circles one is recognized as holding the smaller digit and the other the larger.

constraints.name: x[c]
constraints.intent: For each circle, the digit placed in it equals the digit value selected by its assignment, where each candidate value contributes its own magnitude weighted by whether that value was chosen for the circle.

constraints.name: y[c,
constraints.intent: Each circle is assigned exactly one digit value, so across all candidate values the assignment for a circle sums to one.

constraints.name: y[cc,
constraints.intent: Each digit value is used in exactly one circle, so across all circles the assignment of any given value sums to one.

constraints.name: dummy
constraints.intent: The accounting variable equals the total of all the digits placed across the circles.

query: There's a proposal on the table to keep a couple of the neighbouring circles in a fixed pecking order. Specifically, we'd line up circle c2 so its digit never comes out larger than the one in circle c1. Plug that ordering in and walk me through the placement the optimizer settles on and the digit total.

query: We're looking at a layout where the two outer circles c2 and c4 jointly pull some weight. Suppose we require the digits sitting in circles c2 and c4, added together, to come to at least 7. Does a valid placement still exist, and what does the optimizer return for the overall digit total?

query: There's an idea on the table to avoid a particular swap between the two end circles. We don't want the situation where circle c1 is handed the fourth digit and circle c4 is handed the first digit at the same time — at most one of those two assignment flags should be switched on. What does the optimizer report back for the placement and the digit total under that restriction?

query: Engineering is pitching a rule that keeps at least one strong digit on the leading pair of circles. Across circles c1 and c2 together, we'd want at least one of them to land one of the two highest digit slots rather than letting both pull small numbers. Switch that requirement on and tell me the resulting placement and the digit total.

query: We're evaluating a scenario where the smallest digit is reserved for the busy hub circle c3 and nowhere else. Concretely, every circle apart from c3 would have to carry a digit of 2 or more, leaving c3 as the only one allowed to take the lone digit. What does the optimizer return for the placement and the digit total once that's imposed across the board?

query: Circle c4 sits out on the tail of the chain, yet it keeps ending up with a smaller digit than the more central circle c2. That feels upside down to me — shouldn't the outer circle c4 hold a digit at least as large as c2's, instead of getting undercut by it? What's keeping the model from lining them up that way?

query: The two adjacent circles c2 and c3 between them seem to be soaking up a lot of the high digits, which crowds the big numbers into one corner of the layout. Wouldn't it be tidier to keep their two digits added together down to 5 or less, spreading the weight elsewhere? What's the tradeoff the model is making by piling value onto that pair?

query: It looks odd to me that the leading circle c1 keeps coming out ahead of the trailing circle c4 in value. If anything I'd expect the order reversed down the chain — couldn't we at least require c1's digit to be no larger than c4's? What's the model seeing that makes putting the bigger number up front the right call?

The objective is to minimize an accounting variable that totals the digits placed across all circles.

sets.name: c
sets.doc: the circles in the figure, each of which must hold one digit; also serves as the set of available digit slots since there are exactly as many digits as circles

sets.name: cc
sets.doc: an alias of the circle set, used as the second index when a relationship ranges over pairs of circles or over the eight candidate digit values; the position of a member in this ordering, counting from one, is the digit value it represents

sets.name: net
sets.doc: the pairs of circles that are adjacent in the figure, listed as ordered pairs; the relation is symmetric so each unordered adjacency appears twice, once in each direction

params.name: bign
params.doc: a large constant used to deactivate an adjacency difference inequality when the relevant ordering indicator selects the other direction; a scalar with value 9

params.name: gap
params.doc: the minimum required absolute difference between the digits placed in two adjacent circles; a scalar with value 2

params.name: lo
params.doc: the smallest digit value that may be placed in a circle; a scalar with value 1

params.name: up
params.doc: the largest digit value that may be placed in a circle; a scalar with value 8

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the digit value placed in each circle, an integer bounded between the smallest and largest allowed digit

vars.name: ll
vars.doc: an ordering indicator for an ordered pair of circles; it records which of the two adjacent circles holds the smaller digit, and only one direction of each adjacency can be active

vars.name: y
vars.doc: a digit assignment indicator that equals 1 when a circle is given the digit value corresponding to the second index and 0 otherwise

vars.name: dummy
vars.doc: an accounting variable holding the total of all placed digits

constraints.name: x[cc]
constraints.intent: For each adjacent pair of circles in a given direction, the digit in the second circle must stay below the digit in the first circle by at least the required difference, but only when the ordering indicator for that direction says the first circle holds the larger digit. When the indicator points the other way this requirement is loosened by the big constant so that it places no real limit.

constraints.name: x[cc]
constraints.intent: For each adjacent pair of circles in a given direction, the digit in the second circle must exceed the digit in the first circle by at least the required difference, but only when the ordering indicator for the reverse direction says the first circle holds the smaller digit. When that indicator points the other way this requirement is loosened by the big constant so that it places no real limit.

constraints.name: ll[c,
constraints.intent: For each adjacent pair of circles, exactly one of the two ordering directions is active, so between any two adjacent circles one is recognized as holding the smaller digit and the other the larger.

constraints.name: x[c]
constraints.intent: For each circle, the digit placed in it equals the digit value selected by its assignment, where each candidate value contributes its own magnitude weighted by whether that value was chosen for the circle.

constraints.name: y[c,
constraints.intent: Each circle is assigned exactly one digit value, so across all candidate values the assignment for a circle sums to one.

constraints.name: y[cc,
constraints.intent: Each digit value is used in exactly one circle, so across all circles the assignment of any given value sums to one.

constraints.name: dummy
constraints.intent: The accounting variable equals the total of all the digits placed across the circles.

query: I keep landing on the idea that the busiest hub circle c3 ought to be the anchor that holds the largest digit, yet the plan lets other circles outrank it. Shouldn't no circle be allowed to carry a digit bigger than the one in c3, so the hub stays on top? What's keeping the model from making the hub the high point of the layout?
